edHandoff.summary: 71-year-old male with HTN, DM2, and CKD Stage 3 (baseline Cr 1.6) presenting with 4 days of nausea, poor PO intake, and dizziness. Cr found to be 4.8, BUN 82. K 5.8 (ECG: peaked T-waves, no widening). Given calcium gluconate, insulin/D50, and 1L NS. Foley placed — only 100mL in 2 hours. He takes lisinopril and was recently started on ibuprofen by his PCP for knee pain 10 days ago.
edHandoff.disposition: Admit to medicine for AKI workup and management. Possible nephrology consult.

patient.communicationStyle: Cooperative, somewhat anxious about kidney function. Didn't realize ibuprofen could be harmful. Concerned about dialysis.

chiefComplaint.onset: Progressive over 4-5 days. Started with decreased appetite, then nausea, then dizziness.

present: oliguria (noticed decreased urine output past 2 days)

pmh: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (A1c 7.8%, on insulin + metformin)
Osteoarthritis of bilateral knees (new diagnosis — reason for NSAID)

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.dose: 600mg TID — started 10 days ago by PCP for knee pain

socialHistory.fluidIntake: Poor PO intake past 4-5 days — maybe a few sips of water

physicalExam.general: Elderly male, appears fatigued and mildly volume-depleted. Alert and oriented.
physicalExam.heent: Dry mucous membranes. Decreased skin turgor.
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Mild tachycardia, regular, no murmurs. No S3.
physicalExam.abdominal: Soft, non-tender. No palpable bladder (Foley in place, draining minimal).
physicalExam.extremities: 1+ bilateral pitting edema to ankles (mild). No skin changes.
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert, oriented x4. No asterixis. No myoclonus.

labs.note: BUN:Cr ratio ~17:1 — not classically prerenal (>20:1), but NSAID involvement reduces ratio
Hyperkalemia — ACEi + AKI + reduced renal excretion. ECG showed peaked T-waves.
Non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis — reduced renal acid excretion
Poorly controlled — stress + reduced metformin clearance

labs.value: Specific gravity 1.022, no WBC, no bacteria, trace protein, few hyaline casts, few granular casts
labs.note: Hyaline casts = prerenal. Granular casts suggest some ATN component. No active sediment (no RBC casts, no WBC casts).
Low FeNa and low FEUrea suggest prerenal physiology predominates (kidneys still concentrating — holding onto sodium)

labs.value: Bilateral kidneys 10.5cm (right) and 10.2cm (left). No hydronephrosis. Increased echogenicity bilaterally (consistent with chronic kidney disease). Normal bladder (Foley in place). No masses.

admittingOrders.stopNephrotoxins: DISCONTINUE ibuprofen (primary offender), HOLD lisinopril (worsening AKI by reducing GFR), HOLD metformin (lactic acidosis risk with Cr >1.5, and reduced clearance). These are the THREE critical medication changes.
admittingOrders.ivFluids: NS 250mL/hr x 4 hours, then reassess volume status and UOP. Goal UOP >0.5mL/kg/hr. Be cautious — patient has CKD and may not tolerate aggressive fluids.
admittingOrders.hyperkalemiaManagement: Calcium gluconate already given (cardioprotection). Insulin 10U regular + D50 25g IV (given in ED). Recheck K in 2 hours. Kayexalate 30g PO or Patiromer if K remains >5.5. Avoid repeat calcium if ECG normalizes.
admittingOrders.monitoring: BMP q8h x 24 hours (trending Cr, K, bicarb). Strict I&O. Daily weights. Foley for accurate UOP measurement. Continuous telemetry (hyperkalemia risk). Glucose monitoring q6h (insulin/dextrose + diabetes).
admittingOrders.diet: Renal diet: low potassium, low phosphorus, low sodium. 1.5L fluid restriction initially if edema worsens.
admittingOrders.diabetesManagement: Continue insulin glargine at reduced dose (15 units, given reduced clearance). Sliding scale insulin for correction. Hold metformin until Cr improving.
admittingOrders.consultations: Nephrology consult (KDIGO Stage 3 AKI on CKD, may need to discuss dialysis indications if no improvement in 24-48 hours). If K refractory, BUN uremic symptoms, volume overload, or acidosis worsening → emergent dialysis.
admittingOrders.dvtProphylaxis: SCDs only (hold chemical prophylaxis given AKI — clearance concerns)

keyLearningPoints: The triple whammy: ACEi (efferent dilation) + NSAID (afferent constriction) + volume depletion = catastrophic GFR drop
NSAIDs are CONTRAINDICATED in CKD — they block prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation, the kidney's last compensatory mechanism
KDIGO AKI staging: Stage 1 (1.5-1.9x), Stage 2 (2-2.9x), Stage 3 (≥3x or Cr ≥4.0 or need for RRT). This patient is Stage 3.
FeNa <1% in oliguric patient = prerenal physiology. But FeNa is unreliable on diuretics — use FEUrea instead (<35% = prerenal)
Urine microscopy: hyaline casts = prerenal; muddy brown granular casts = ATN; RBC casts = glomerulonephritis. This patient has overlap.
Always check a renal ultrasound to rule out obstruction — it's the most easily reversible cause of AKI
mustNotMiss: Post-renal obstruction (US normal here, Foley draining — ruled out)
Urgent dialysis indications (AEIOU): Acidosis refractory, Electrolytes (refractory hyperK), Ingestion, Overload (volume), Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)
Glomerulonephritis (active urine sediment — RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs)
commonPitfalls: Not stopping the NSAID — this is the single most important intervention
Not holding the ACEi — it's worsening the hemodynamic insult
Continuing metformin in AKI — risk of severe lactic acidosis
Over-resuscitating a CKD patient — they can't handle the volume. Use clinical endpoints.
Forgetting to check urine studies before giving IV fluids (dilutes the urine values)
Not checking a renal ultrasound to exclude obstruction
teaching.attendingQuestions: This patient's creatinine tripled. Walk me through the causes of AKI systematically — prerenal, intrinsic, postrenal. Then tell me: which medications caused this, and why does the combination matter? What's the 'triple whammy'?
